learning_outcomes.description: Understand the role and use of system models: Concepts of modelling and the use of models in the product/process life cycle: Understand why modelling is done in industry and consultancy and where it is applied across the life cycle. Appreciate what models allow you to do in terms of decision making.
Understand the role and use of system models: Have the ability to observe particular engineered or natural systems for their dynamic behaviour and relate this to underlying physics and chemistry.
Understand the role and use of system models: Understand how to evaluate dynamic behaviour to prevent critical system failure. This prevents environmental, social, and safety violations.
Model development strategy and framework: Understand and apply a systematic model development framework applied to a range of relevant problems.
Model development strategy and framework: Development of relevant phenomenological models through the application of basic physics and chemistry.
Model development strategy and framework: Develop an empirical model from input-output plant data time series and apply the model to an application area.
Analysis and solution techniques: An understanding of the structural characteristics of models and the implications on solution approaches.
Analysis and solution techniques: The ability to translate model descriptions into executable programs.
Analysis and solution techniques: The ability to critically analyse written code, debug it and obtain dynamic trend predictions.
Application of a model in process control: Develop the capability to quantitatively describe the behaviour of simple control systems and design effective control systems.
Application of a model in process control: Cultivate proficiency in utilising computer software to facilitate the description and design of control systems.
Application of a model in process control: Develop techniques for tuning controllers to achieve desired system performance, with opportunities to apply this knowledge in laboratory settings.

assessments.task_description:
You will conduct an experiment involving the observation and collection of data from a dynamic system (distillation column). These will be held during week 3. Each group will be assigned to a particular timeslot and experiment. A brief report is required to be submitted one week after completing the lab. Further details available on the course blackboard site.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.due_date:
10/08/2026 9:00 am

Distillation column induction module will be available via Blackboard
assessments.task_description:
Pre-Lab Induction: Distillation Column (complete before your Week 3 practical)

Before you operate the distillation column, you must complete this induction. It walks you through the real rig from the feed tank, through the column where the separation happens, to the two product tanks and explains how the column works, how it's kept safe and stable, and what you'll be doing in the lab.

More details will be posted in course Blackboard


After the induction: You will attend the practical at the real pilot plant during Week 3, where you'll operate the column and collect data yourself taking samples from the feed, distillate and bottoms streams and measuring their compositions on the refractometer under different feed flow rates. This induction is your preparation for that session, so arrive already familiar with the equipment, the safety trips, and what you'll be measuring. The data you collect will underpin the modelling and control work you build across the rest of the course.


Completion of Distillation Induction Module is a mandatory prerequisite for the Week 3 distillation practical. You must pass the induction gate check before the practical session. Retakes are unlimited, but the module must be passed before your allocated practical time. Access to the practical including the lab worksheet and data-entry sheet is released through Blackboard only once you have passed; students who have not passed will not be admitted to the pilot plant, as this is a safety requirement for operating the equipment.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Students must attend the practical to obtain the data sets needed for the project assignment.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You cannot defer or apply for an extension for this assessment.

7/09/2026 1:00 pm

25/09/2026 1:00 pm

Submission: The report should be submitted to Gradescope in Blackboard.
assessments.category: Computer Code, Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Project
assessments.task_description:
Part 1: Model Development and Analysis: Develop first-principles grey-box model of the distillation column (mass and energy balances per stage, with closing rate and equilibrium relations), following the method from weekly tutorials. Analyse the equation set: state your assumptions, identify states and parameters, perform a degrees-of-freedom analysis, and justify which parameters must be fitted rather than predicted. The model is not solved numerically in this part.

Part 2: Model solution, validation and application: Implement and numerically solve the Part 1 model in Python. Calibrate the model by estimating the reboiler heat-transfer parameter, a single physical parameter fitted by least squares to your measured composition data across all runs. Validate the model by predicting an independent, unfitted quantity, then apply it to predict untested feed-flow conditions and to provide the plant model for Part 3.

Each part of the project is weighted at 15% each.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Submission: The report should be submitted to Gradescope in Blackboard.

assessments.category: Computer Code, Paper/ Report/ Annotation, Project
assessments.task_description:
Part 3: Empirical Modelling and PID Control: From the bottoms-composition step response to reflux ratio changes, identify a first-order-plus-dead-time (FOPDT) model. Use it to design and tune a PID controller that holds the bottoms composition at setpoint within the acceptable product range, using a systematic tuning method. Evaluate closed-loop setpoint tracking, disturbance rejection, and stability, justifying your choices. Additionally, you will assess various combinations of advanced control arrangements and fine-tune the control parameters.

Each part of the project is weighted at 15% each.

Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Translation (MT) are emerging tools that may support students in completing this assessment task. Students may appropriately use AI and/or MT in completing this assessment task. Students must clearly reference any use of AI or MT in each instance. A failure to reference AI use may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.

assessments.task_description:
This will be three-hour theory/paper exam covering all concepts learned throughout the semester.

The exam will be in person, invigilated, and closed book.

This assessment task evaluates students' abilities, skills and knowledge without the aid of generative Artificial Intelligence (AI) or Machine Translation (MT). Students are advised that the use of AI or MT technologies to develop responses is strictly prohibited and may constitute student misconduct under the Student Code of Conduct.
